segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a tablet and laptop displaying a Go game, with a hand interacting with the tablet, suggesting digital engagement with the ancient board game. The scene transitions to Nicholas Thompson, Editor-in-Chief of Wired, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, his expression serious and thoughtful. The narrative then shifts to the Google DeepMind Challenge Match, where Lee Se-dol, the South Korean Go grandmaster, is shown playing against AlphaGo. He is seated at a wooden Go board, wearing a dark suit, deeply focused, with a monitor displaying the game beside him. The broadcast screen shows the match details, including timers for Lee Se-dol and AlphaGo, and a commentator exclaims, 'Oh, totally unthinkable move,' highlighting a pivotal moment. Lee Se-dol’s reactions—ranging from intense concentration to contemplative gestures—convey the psychological weight of the match. The scene includes a wide shot of the match setup, with the AlphaGo team observing from behind a blue partition, and a split-screen view showing the game and commentators. The segment concludes with Kai-Fu Lee, an AI scientist, speaking in an interview setting, his expression calm and analytical, providing expert commentary on the implications of the match.

segments.caption: The video opens with a sweeping aerial view of the Golden Gate Bridge at dusk, showcasing its iconic orange towers and the San Francisco skyline in the distance, with sailboats gliding beneath the span. Inside, a white humanoid robot with a red scarf and a tablet displaying a maze-like pattern stands near a window with a view of the Bay Bridge. The camera then cuts to a security monitor displaying live feeds from a Knightscope robot, showing the girl’s face in a close-up view. The scene shifts to a dimly lit auditorium where Jaron Lanier, identified as Chief Technology Officer and author, stands on stage for a talk titled 'DIGITAL DIGNITY' with Nicholas Thompson of WIRED. The video then cuts to a close-up of Lanier speaking, followed by a montage of WIRED magazine covers from 1997 to 2014, featuring bold designs and notable figures like Edward Snowden, emphasizing the magazine’s evolution and focus on technology and digital culture. The segment ends with a return to Lanier’s close-up, reinforcing his role as a central speaker.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of multiple white surveillance cameras mounted on a black pole, set against a blurred urban backdrop with illuminated signage, establishing a theme of pervasive monitoring. The scene transitions to a large public digital screen displaying a real-time surveillance interface with Chinese text, including '涉嫌违法人员' (suspected lawbreakers) and '实时视频' (live video), showing blurred images of pedestrians and a traffic light. The screen then flashes a warning: '您已涉嫌违法!' (You are suspected of violating the law!), accompanied by a blurred face and a red light, while pedestrians walk by, some glancing at the screen. The setting shifts to an indoor interview with Xiao Qiang, a research scientist from UC Berkeley, who speaks expressively in a well-lit room with white doors and windows in the background, wearing a gray blazer over a black shirt. The video then cuts to a tech exhibition, where a white surveillance camera with 'Face++' branding is shown, followed by a large screen displaying AI-powered facial recognition software identifying attendees in real-time, with green boxes highlighting faces and Chinese text indicating names and timestamps. The final shot shows a live street surveillance feed with AI overlays identifying individuals by gender, clothing, and movement, with Chinese labels such as '男' (male) and '短发' (short hair), demonstrating advanced real-time tracking capabilities.

segments.caption: The video opens with a formal handshake between Xi Jinping and a Senegalese official, both in dark suits, set against a backdrop of palm trees and a red-paved area, indicating a diplomatic meeting. The scene transitions to the exterior of the 'ARENE NATIONALE DE LUTTE' in Senegal, a modern stadium with a curved roof, where crowds gather and flags of China and Senegal are displayed. segments.caption: The video segment transitions into a closing credit sequence for the FRONTLINE documentary 'In the Age of AI'. The background is a dark, abstract digital environment with faint, blurred data streams and light flares, creating a high-tech, cinematic atmosphere. The FRONTLINE logo appears in bold white text on a red background in the upper left corner, establishing the program's identity. The credits roll vertically, listing key contributors such as the directors, writers, producers, editors, and crew, with names like Neil Docherty, David Fanning, Michele Hozer, and others appearing in clean white text. Funding acknowledgments are displayed, including the MacArthur Foundation, Ford Foundation, Abrams Foundation, Park Foundation, FRONTLINE Journalism Fund, and Corporation for Public Broadcasting, with their respective logos shown in sequence. A URL, 'pbs.org/frontline', appears on the right side for viewers to access more information. As the credits continue, a DVD cover for 'In the Age of AI' is shown on the right, priced at $24.99 plus S&H, with purchase options via shopPBS.org and availability on Prime Video. The final credits include executive producers and legal information, concluding with a copyright notice for © 2017 Dow Jones & Company Inc. The overall tone is professional and polished, emphasizing the documentary’s production quality and institutional support.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dark, digital-themed credit sequence for FRONTLINE, featuring a red and blue lens flare effect over a blurred background of data streams and computer interfaces. The text 'FRONTLINE' appears in bold white letters on a red rectangle in the upper-left corner, followed by the executive producer's name, Raney Aronson-Rath. The sequence transitions to display 'A FRONTLINE production with Five O’Clock Films' in white text against a red horizontal light streak. Subsequently, copyright information is shown: '© 2019 WGBH Educational Foundation ALL RIGHTS RESERVED' and 'FRONTLINE is a production of WGBH/Boston, which is solely responsible for its content,' all set against the same dark, data-filled backdrop. The segment concludes with a bright, fiery orange and yellow animation of the 'WGBH' logo, which glows and pulses with light before fading to black. The overall atmosphere is sleek, high-tech, and professional, emphasizing the documentary's production quality and institutional backing.
